note_data.title: Computational thinking and problem-solving
keyPoints: **Big-O notation** describes the worst-case time complexity of an algorithm as input size increases
**O(1)** represents constant time complexity - execution time remains the same regardless of input size
**O(n)** represents linear time complexity - execution time increases proportionally with input size
**O(n²)** represents quadratic time complexity - execution time increases with the square of input size
**Recursion** is a programming technique where a function calls itself with a modified parameter until a base case is reached
**Divide and conquer** breaks a problem into smaller subproblems, solves them independently, then combines results
**Backtracking** systematically explores all possible solutions and backtracks when a path leads to an invalid solution
**Base case** in recursion prevents infinite loops by providing a condition where the function stops calling itself
**Merge sort** and **quicksort** are classic examples of divide and conquer algorithms with O(n log n) complexity
**Stack overflow** occurs when recursive calls exceed the system's stack memory limit

definitions.definition: A mathematical notation that describes the limiting behavior of an algorithm's time or space complexity as the input size approaches infinity
A programming technique where a function calls itself with modified parameters until a base case condition is met
An algorithmic paradigm that breaks a problem into smaller subproblems of the same type, solves them recursively, then combines their solutions
A systematic method for solving problems by exploring all possible solutions and undoing choices that lead to invalid solutions
The terminating condition in a recursive function that stops further recursive calls and begins the return process

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the Big-O notation or algorithm name only. No explanation needed.
Give the algorithm description + how it works using 'because' or 'this means that'
State both time complexities and explain which is more efficient using Big-O notation
Show each step of the recursive calls with parameter values and return values

modelAnswer.question: Explain how the divide and conquer approach works and give one advantage of using this approach. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Divide and conquer works by breaking a large problem into smaller subproblems of the same type. Each subproblem is solved independently, often using recursion. The solutions to the subproblems are then combined to solve the original problem. One advantage is improved efficiency because it often reduces time complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n) for sorting algorithms.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Breaking problem into smaller subproblems
Mark 4: Advantage with specific example of efficiency improvement

commonMistakes: Never say 'Big-O shows speed' — always say 'Big-O shows time complexity or growth rate'
Students often forget to include the base case when explaining recursion
Confusing backtracking with simple recursion — backtracking specifically undoes invalid choices
Writing O(n) as 'Order n' instead of 'Big-O of n' or 'O of n'
Not showing the combining step when explaining divide and conquer
examinerTips: Big-O notation questions appear in every Paper 2 - learn O(1), O(n), O(n²), O(log n), O(n log n)
Always trace recursive calls step by step showing parameter changes
Divide and conquer questions often ask for merge sort or binary search examples
Backtracking questions typically involve maze solving or N-Queens problem
formulas: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) for divide and conquer
Space complexity for recursion = O(depth of recursion)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Binary tree diagram showing recursive calls with parameters at each node, demonstrating how problems are divided and solutions combined back up the tree
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain decomposition and abstraction using a real-world example of designing a school management system
N/D 2022 P2 Q2 (8 marks) — candidates had to identify pattern recognition and algorithmic thinking in a given flowchart for calculating exam grades
M/J 2022 P1 Q3 (4 marks) — students were required to apply decomposition to break down the problem of organizing a school sports day event
N/D 2023 P2 Q1 (5 marks) — question focused on using abstraction to model a library booking system, removing unnecessary details

workedExample.question: A school wants to create a system for managing student attendance. Using computational thinking, explain how you would approach this problem. Include decomposition, pattern recognition, abstraction, and algorithmic thinking in your answer. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Decomposition - Break down into smaller parts: student registration, daily attendance recording, absence tracking, report generation
Step 2: Pattern recognition - Identify recurring elements: daily check-in process, weekly/monthly reporting cycles, similar data structure for all students
Step 3: Abstraction - Remove unnecessary details: focus on essential data (student ID, date, present/absent) rather than irrelevant information like student hobbies
Step 4: Algorithmic thinking - Create step-by-step solution: 1) Display student list 2) Mark each as present/absent 3) Save to database 4) Generate reports when requested
workedExample.answer: Decomposition: Break the system into modules - student registration, attendance recording, absence tracking, and report generation. Pattern recognition: Identify that attendance follows daily patterns and reporting follows regular cycles. Abstraction: Focus only on essential data (student ID, name, date, attendance status) while ignoring irrelevant details. Algorithmic thinking: Create a logical sequence - display student list, record attendance, validate data, save to database, and generate reports on demand.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correctly explaining decomposition with example, 2 marks for pattern recognition with relevant patterns identified, 2 marks for abstraction showing what to include/exclude, 2 marks for algorithmic thinking with clear logical steps

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused decomposition with simply listing features' — always show how you break down a complex problem into smaller, manageable sub-problems when explaining decomposition

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often failed to distinguish between abstraction and generalization' — remember abstraction means removing unnecessary details while keeping essential features, not making something more general

Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak responses to algorithmic thinking questions lacked clear sequencing' — always present algorithmic thinking as numbered, logical steps that could be followed by someone else
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a computational thinking cycle diagram showing four interconnected boxes: 'Decomposition' (breaking down problems), 'Pattern Recognition' (identifying similarities), 'Abstraction' (focusing on essential features), and 'Algorithmic Thinking' (creating step-by-step solutions). Arrows should connect these in a cycle, with a central 'Problem' box, demonstrating how these thinking skills work together iteratively to solve complex problems.
note_data.quickRecap: Computational thinking uses four key skills: decomposition (breaking problems into smaller parts), pattern recognition (spotting similarities and trends), abstraction (focusing on essential details only), and algorithmic thinking (creating step-by-step solutions). These skills work together to solve complex problems systematically. Always provide specific examples when explaining each skill, and remember that algorithmic thinking must show clear, logical sequences that others can follow.
